text: The robotic system targets the hepatic flexure, focusing on this specific area of the colon for surgical intervention.
The robotic setup includes bipolar forceps in the first arm, scissors in the third arm, a tip-up clamp in the fourth arm, and the second arm is utilized for optimal camera positioning.
The surgeon gently lifts the right colon upwards, revealing the alocholic vessels beneath.
The surgeon identifies the presumed location of the heliocolic vessels and begins dissection using a medial to lateral approach, with visible manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the aneocolic vein and artery, using the third robotic arm equipped with a clip applier to secure these vessels.
The surgeon clips the vessels at their origin using amalocs and then cuts them, with robotic arms assisting in the precise manipulation of instruments.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection of the right colon, using robotic arms equipped with bipolar forceps, scissors, and a tip-up clamp to manipulate and separate the tissue layers.
After completing the medial dissection, the surgeon places a swab cranially to maintain exposure and control any potential bleeding.
The surgeon uses a robotic endo-vessel sealer in the third arm to divide the mesentery of the distal ileum, separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection along the embryologic plane, using robotic instruments to carefully separate tissue layers.
The surgeon divides adhesions between the omentum and the transverse colon, revealing the tattoo marking on the colon surface.
The surgeon divides the omentum, separating it from adjacent structures using surgical instruments.
The swab placed after the medial dissection is visible, positioned to protect the duodenum and surrounding vessels from potential injury during the procedure.
Following the completion of lateral dissection and the takedown of the hepatic flexure, the surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the mesocolon, visibly separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a DII colectomy, continuing the procedure after identifying the tattoo and completing the lateral dissection along the embryologic plane.
The surgeon sections the ileocolic artery and vein at their origin near the superior mesenteric vein and artery, completing the vascular division in this area.
The surgical field shows the absence of the right colic artery and vein, indicating their anatomical variation or previous ligation.
The surgeon sections the right branch of the middle colic vein and artery at their origin, completing the vascular dissection in the surgical field.
The surgeon removes lymphoadipose tissue covering the superior mesenteric vein and artery, as well as the middle colic vessels, revealing these structures during the DII colectomy.
The surgeon performs dissection and resection of the lymphoadipose tissue covering the superior mesenteric vein, targeting nodal group II-I-VI, and proceeds with the dissection of Henlein's trunk.
The surgeon completes the dissection and divides the transverse colon and terminal ileum using an endostepla instrument, which is positioned in the third robotic arm.
The surgeon performs dissection and release of the second adrenal portion, revealing the renal pedicle beneath.
The surgeon performs dissection around the renal pedicle to visually identify the right renal artery, preparing it for future clamping.
The surgeon exposes the inferior pole of the kidney, identifying the margins of the lesion and marking them with diathermy.
The surgeon clamps the renal artery, initiating visible ischemia in the kidney tissue.
The surgeon performs a tumorectomy, excising the tumor with macroscopically healthy tissue margins visible around the resection site.
The surgeon performs a hemostatic continuous barbed suture using Hemolox to reduce ischemia in the kidney, following the clamping of the renal artery.
The surgeon removes the arterial clamp from the right renal artery, restoring blood flow to the kidney.
The surgeon checks for correct kidney reperfusion and confirms the absence of active bleeding before removing the specimen. The specimen is then placed into a bag for retrieval.
The surgeon applies a hemostatic agent to the site of the tumorectomy, ensuring control of any potential bleeding at the resection margins.
Indocyanine green dye is applied to assess the vascularization of the ileum and colon, highlighting the blood supply to these areas in preparation for the anastomosis.
The robotic surgical system is configured with bipolar forceps in the first arm, a needle holder in the third arm, a tip-up instrument in the fourth arm, and the camera positioned in the second arm.
The surgeon begins posterior reinforcement of the anastomosis site using a running three-hole absorbable suture, ensuring secure closure of the tissue.
The surgeon applies a barber's suture technique, visually securing the tissue edges with a continuous stitch pattern.
After completing the posterior suture, the surgeon creates two enterotomies in the ileum and colon, preparing for the ileocolic anastomosis.
The assistant surgeon uses a suction device to clear fluids from the surgical field, ensuring visibility and cleanliness during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis using a surgical stapler, aligning the bowel segments for secure attachment.
The surgeon places the endostaple into the third robotic arm, preparing for its use in the procedure.
The surgeon closes the defect using single absorbable three-hole wiper stitches, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated securely.
The assistant surgeon uses scissors to perform a precise cutting action on the tissue.
The surgeon performs anterior reinforcement using a running three-hole absorbable barbed suture, securing the tissue with precise stitching.
The surgeon uses the epiprone to cover the staple side-to-side anastomosis, ensuring the anastomotic site is shielded.
Two specimens are placed in separate endo bags and extracted through a funnestine incision.
